data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Jackson Hole, NZ Rate Decisions And U.K./EU Inflation Data
data.description:
By Elior Manier

After an already volatile trading week, next week will see a crossroads of data and geopolitical catalysts that may move currency, equity, and crypto markets.

Before looking at those, let's take a look at what happened this week.

Week in review: US mixed inflation data sending warning signs, RBA rate cut, and Trump-Putin meeting

The US path to a much anticipated rate cut was well-drawn, particularly after Tuesday's CPI report coming along the Fed's inflation target (2.5% y/y on the headline, 2.7% on the core.)

However, Thursday's PPI data changed the narrative quite a lot. Coming in at 0.9% vs. 0.2% expected (and bringing the y/y core to 3.7%!), markets caught a bad surprise: tariff-led inflation is making its way to the US data.

With a mix of downward revised NFP data and a not-so-clear US inflation outlook, the September rate cut isn't going to be such a given. There will still be one more NFP report and CPI report before the 17th of September meeting happens.

In the meantime, risk assets and sentiment have taken a hit from the tariff fears: cryptos have taken a tough hit, and US indices are a bit more indecisive yet still holding close to their recent highs.

Weekly performance from different asset classes

The Trump-Putin meeting should also be making headlines very soon, with the two presidents starting discussions in the afternoon. The Kremlin spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, expects a long meeting, saying that "In general, we can imagine that it will take at least 6-7 hours."

Access our WTI Oil analysis as some heightened volatility can be expected there in the afternoon, with volatility in the commodity possibly also spilling on Monday. Elsewhere, markets saw the Royal Bank of Australia cutting rates to 3.60% on mixed signals for the future RBA outlook.

The Week Ahead: Jackson Hole Symposium and a lot more

One of the key events concerning all types of markets is the yearly Jackson Hole Symposium. A roadmap for the event just got published. For those who don't know, the Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium is an annual gathering hosted by the Kansas City Fed, bringing together central bankers, policymakers, academics, and market participants to discuss key economic issues.

While the topics vary each year, it's closely watched for views from central banks on the future outlook with tariff concerns and global growth.

One of the most memorable market-shaking moments from the yearly conference came in 2010, when then-Fed Chair Ben Bernanke used his Jackson Hole speech to signal a second round of quantitative easing (QE2).

The hint sent risk assets surging and Treasury yields lower, cementing Jackson Hole's reputation as a stage for major policy signals.

Asia Pacific Markets: Royal Bank of New Zealand rate decision

Asian-Pacific markets will not get too much in terms of key data, with the main event happening in New Zealand. The RBNZ is widely expected to cut their rates to 3% from 3.25%. Keep an eye on our upcoming RBNZ meeting preview coming up on Monday. The rate decision is happening on Tuesday at 22:00 ET.

For the rest, mid-tier data releases include Japan and New Zealand's trade balance, the Japanese national CPI data, and Australian PMIs. Markets are also awaiting the PBoC rate decision, just before the RBNZ meeting.

The Chinese economy has been stagnant for a while now, except for the stimulus offered by the central bank. Economists are waiting to see if there is more stimulus to come to boost notably APAC currencies (like AUD and NZD) and commodities' growth outlook.

US, Europe, and UK markets: PMI data, Canadian inflation, Eurozone, and UK PPI releases

The week really starts on Tuesday with the Canadian inflation forecasted at 2%. We will see if the Loonie gets enough of a push to strengthen after a rough past week. The CAD has been getting dragged around by US dollar flows, with the USD/CAD stuck in consolidation.

Markets will also see if the UK inflation gets a boost, which may confirm further the doubts of prolonged rate cuts from the Bank of England. The BoE conference on Thursday, August 7th, had almost failed to deliver a cut.

In Europe, both the UK and Germany will release their PPI data on Wednesday, where we will see how producer prices moved on the other side of the Atlantic. They might be movers for European currencies after this week's market shaking US PPI.

The US doesn't have many economic releases per se, but will still see a few events, including the FOMC Minutes on Wednesday (14:00) and many key speeches from a divided Federal Reserve, including Jerome Powell in Jackson Hole scheduled at 10:00 AM on Friday.

Of course, don't forget to check all the different PMI releases expected from the Eurozone, the UK, and finally the US (Thursday 9:45 for US global services PMI).

Safe trades and enjoy your weekend!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SoundHound: Likely To Take Off After Raising FY 2025 Guidance
data.description:
The last time I covered SoundHound AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOUN) was back in March, during the market meltdown. Naturally, I rated this stock as a strong sell, as fundamentals and valuation suddenly begin to matter more than the narrative or the growth when the broader markets are contracting. In these times, funds tend to derisk their portfolios, and more often than not, stocks with sky-high valuations tend to be the first ones to go.

With the markets back at all-time highs, I considered reviewing speculative, high-growth stocks, like SoundHound. I admit that I was quite surprised after the company crushed Q2 earnings expectations and increased its FY 2025 revenue guidance. At the mid-point of that guidance ($167 million), the company is essentially targeting a triple-digit growth this year (well, a 98.8% to be specific).

The growth story, especially in the QSR segment, remains strong. Margins improved sequentially to 58% non-GAAP (39% GAAP), and the CFO is reviewing low margin contracts. Speaking of which, there is a risk that could make near-term revenue a bit lumpy, as some restaurant deals renew and volume discounts may no longer be available.

Overall, I see upside ahead, mainly in the QSR segment, with an attractive price action following a breakout after Q2 earnings.

Why I Wasn't Wrong With My Strong Sell Rating

On March 13, 2025, my article titled "SoundHound: Spectacular Q4 Earnings Can't Drown Out The Broader Market Selloff" was published on SA.

If you have been reading my previous articles, you know that I pay little to no attention to valuation analysis for most (but not all) stocks when the broader markets are expanding. Notice this is not the case during a broader market contraction or stagnation. Why?

Excess liquidity. I won't go into the details here, but I call excess liquidity when there’s more cash or credit looking for a home than the real economy or safe assets can easily absorb at current rates, so it spills into risk assets like equities. The opposite is also true: when the broader markets are selling off due to deteriorating economics or the fear in anticipation of a deteriorating economic landscape, market participants typically deleverage and unload the most speculative assets in their portfolios (I often inverse my long positions and go short).

Well, that's exactly what happened in Q1 and part of Q2 this year. In December last year and January this year, I was bullish on SoundHound, as I hadn’t seen any signs of deterioration in the US economy. Therefore, its customer growth, particularly in the QSR segment, along with the triple-digit revenue growth, caught my attention, even if the company was burning cash and had a stretched valuation.

A quick side note: it is my strong belief as an active market participant that in times of an expansionary economy, most stocks that have an attractive growth story and/or fundamentals thrive, regardless (up to a point, of course) of poor valuation or cash burn. For me, if a company doesn’t have any major drags (think Intel’s Foundry segment) weighing on sentiment, and it can grow revenue consistently in the double digits (SoundHound has been growing revenue in the triple digits since Q4 last year), it's generally well-positioned. Of course, there are more angles to my analysis, but that’s the 20% that drives 80% of my rationale (think Pareto).

Then, Trump began teasing his reciprocal tariff agenda, and I turned bearish on most risk assets (i.e., US public equities). SoundHound was one of them, trading at a TTM P/S ratio of 39 and a TTM P/B of 21 when I rated it a strong sell.

Was I right with my rating? In my view, the answer is yes, even if it didn't hit my target (high $3s). Let me explain.

Between March 13 and April 7, the stock plunged an eye watering 28%. If that drop is not enough for a strong sell rating, you tell me what is in the comment section.

Do you understand now why I run away from high beta stocks during a selloff? That's why I said in my March coverage:

I anticipate the SPY will rock bottom at around $480, with speculative AI stocks like SoundHound likely experiencing steeper losses due to their high beta.

Of course, with the pause of reciprocal tariffs on April 9, the economic landscape suddenly improved, businesses gained some certainty around trade policies, and pretty much every US equity regained its momentum prior to the tariff-driven selloff.

So, why didn't you review your rating? (some readers may rightfully say)

I’ll be as honest, transparent, and direct as I can: the day only has so many hours, and even if I wished I could stop time, its passage is inevitable. I don't have a team of analysts to help me cover all the stocks in my universe. Therefore, I need to be hyper-selective with the stocks that I analyze based on the macro and industry trends. After the April 9 selloff, my attention was mostly focused on semiconductor stocks and mega caps. SoundHound didn't really emerge on my radar until now.

Would I have reconsidered my rating on SoundHound on April 9? Unequivocally, yes. That’s water under the bridge. Let’s focus on the present.

An AI Growth Stock

Q2 2025 revenue came in at $42.7 million, up 217% from a year ago, making it the all-time strongest quarter for the company, with growth in all key business units.

As seen above, the segment that contributes the most to revenue is hosted services, with +140% yoy growth in Q2.

Furthermore, the company raised its revenue outlook for 2025, now projecting $160 million to $178 million in revenue. Furthermore, they expect revenue to ramp up as the year exits, targeting adjusted EBITDA profitability exiting 2025.

All that said, we believe we can achieve adjusted EBITDA profitability as we exit 2025. - CFO Nitesh Sharan, Q2 2025 earnings call

In terms of margins, the company achieved a GAAP gross margin of 39% and a non-GAAP gross margin of 58% in Q2, both up sequentially. However, as seen below, the trend is anything but encouraging.

The CFO expects to realize more cost synergies, including cloud synergies, in the coming quarters, where he also noted a decline in like-for-like inference costs. Most importantly, he is evaluating low-margin contracts.

And we are still evaluating low margin contracts as they come up for renewal and identify other areas to improve efficiency.

I believe there could be a major risk here. According to the statement above, those low margin contracts (I believe they have volume-based discounts) are in for a renewal. I cannot help but wonder if their customers in the restaurant segment (a very low margin business) signed up mainly due to these discounts. If they have to pay full price, I believe we could see some revenue lumpiness ahead. In fact, the CFO already hinted at some volatility ahead:

While we hope to repeat that each and every quarter, sometimes the lumpy nature of these deals can cause quarter-to-quarter volatility.

As for customer concentration risk, no customer accounted for more than 10% of the total revenue through the first half of 2025. The list of customers is quite impressive. From what I read in the earnings call, new wins in Q2 include IHOP, Red Lobster, and Peter Piper Pizza. Also, MOD Pizza renewed its contract, Red Robin added Smart Answering, and there were expansions at Chipotle, as well as further rollouts at Firehouse Subs, Habit Burger, McAlister’s Deli, Beef O’Brady’s, and Casey’s. SoundHound is scaling its operations, with an active network of over 14,000 restaurants, 1,000 new locations, and approximately 1M new interactions in Q2.

I have to say that I like the growth in the QSR business, but I would like to see a revenue breakdown per business type (i.e., restaurant vs non-restaurant) to gauge which of the segments has better traction. Unfortunately, it's too early for SoundHound to reveal those figures (as competitors could focus on the higher growth segments and disregard the others).

Speaking of other business units, the drive-thru business is picking up steam, with hundreds of live sites and a strong ecosystem of partners (i.e., Par, Samsung, and HME). Furthermore, the company is mitigating the 6 to 12-month retrofit delay for customers by offering smaller footprint posts (i.e., tablet + mic) in the drive-thru lanes. The results show that average order times are coming down from a minute plus to less than a minute.

In Retail, the company has deals with two multi-hundred unit personal-care companies and a home services company with more than 500 locations. As for the financial services vertical, 7 of the top 10 global FIs are customers, and 4 of them have renewed or were upsold. Furthermore, the automated conversation volume in this sector is doubling year over year, with expansion across regional banks and credit unions.

Moving on to the automotive segment, the company has launched its Chat AI solution in North America, along with three additional global automotive brands. Additionally, they secured several new deals, including a large-scale program in China, representing "millions of units".

Interestingly enough, it seems that ASPs (and therefore, ARPUs, average revenue per user) are growing with the new gen AI features deployed:

In automotive, we signed a new OEM in China and saw a double-digit increase in ASPs primarily from further expansion of our Generative AI solutions.

Finally, from a cash perspective, SoundHound ended the quarter with $230 million in cash and cash equivalents and no debt. As seen below, the cash burn in the last 12 months has surpassed $110 million.

Therefore, SoundHound has a cash runway of little over two years. Unless I see shares trading back in the mid-$20s, I wouldn't worry too much about dilution. That said, there are big risks to consider with this stock.

Risks

As I noted before, revenue may be a little volatile as lower-margin contracts are up for renewal. I believe SoundHound will either completely eliminate these volume discounts or reduce them drastically. Considering the low margins of QSR restaurants (see below Red Robin's EBITDA margin), I believe some may not renew contracts if discounts are completely eliminated.

In fact, churn was one of the topics discussed during the last earnings call:

where there maybe previously had been leakage and some churn that we're refining that we're turning that around into -- from reductions into maintenance to actually growth.

I recommend keeping a close eye on this topic, especially as lower margin contracts are due for renewal (possibly without volume based discounts). The CFO emphasized in the last earnings call that momentum won't be linear.

We have said before that our momentum won't be linear, and we are moving fast to capture the underlying market shifts, which are clearly in our favor.

Speaking of momentum in revenue, it seems that growth this year will be backloaded to Q4 rather than Q3 due to seasonality.

Other risks come from a significant increase in stock based compensation. Over the past 12 months, the company SBC accounted for $60.2 million. That's 82% more than in FY 2024. I am not particularly encouraged about the recent increase; however, the company is growing revenue in the triple digits, so for now, I see this hike in SBC as partially justified.

Valuation

Considering that SoundHound is in the early stages of its growth phase, we have just a few valuation ratios to look at. On a P/S basis, the company is trading at 40x next year's earnings. That is quite a premium when compared to the sector median of just 3.17x.

On a P/E basis, I believe it's too early to talk about profitability. Even the Street is not forecasting a positive EPS over the next couple of years.

Therefore, this is an expensive stock, and value investors should consider this as a risk.

Looking Ahead

SoundHound surprised the revenue consensus in the past quarter by about $9.8 million more than initially expected, but most importantly, it raised FY 2025 guidance. At the mid-point ($167 million), we are talking about a 98.8% yoy increase, almost in the triple digits.

The growth is undeniable, and SoundHound is one of the few players that trade publicly in the US, offering AI services. We are in the middle of an AI supercycle, which I believe could well last until the end of the decade. This, in addition to favorable price action after the recent breakout post earnings, makes me rate SoundHound as a speculative buy. I would keep an eye on customer renewals, particularly in the QSR segment, as those low-margin contracts come to an end.

data.title: Claritev's Stock Performance Could Be Shooting Past Q2 Performance: I Choose A Hold Rating
data.description:
Claritev (NYSE:CTEV) released its Q2 2O25 numbers on August 6 and since then the stock's price which has been on an upward trend has even been more bullish since the latest financials. In just under two weeks, the stock has surged by over 35% and in my view this performance is almost scary. Yes, Q2 has been a good quarter for the company but given the other risks facing the group, now including negative equity, I don't think the level at which the stock price is trading at will be sustainable in the near term. I wouldn't recommend investors to keep accumulating CTEV stock. The best I would do in my view is to HOLD at this point.

What Q2 Performance Looked Like

The provider of healthcare tech-enabled solutions saw revenue rising 3.5% year-on-year (YoY) to $241.6 million whilst EBITDA margins expanded to 63.8%. The revenue surge was also higher than last quarter's level at $231.3 million. That said, it is important to take note that the quarterly revenue figure was also buoyed by a $5 million non-recurring revenue from a new commercial arrangement in its Property & Casualty (P&C) business.

What that means is that that additional revenue does not represent a sustainable recurring revenue. Management also highlighted that it expects a total of roughly $15 million in similar one-off P&C revenue for FY25, with comparable contributions expected in Q3 and Q4.

Besides the $5 million P&C figure, Claritev still managed to record revenue gains for both YoY and from previous quarter. I think that top line performance is quite commendable for a year dubbed as the 'Year of the Turn'.

Positives to Support 'Year of the Turn' Narrative

Claritev has had a successful Q2 after attaining strong client retention and expansion wins. The company managed to renew a top five client and a top 20 client early. Both retentions are for five-year terms.

On top of that, the company managed to close an $81 million+ 10-year subscription deal for Ben Insights. Ben Insights is one of Claritev’s data and decision science products. This deal is the largest in the company's history and carries additional upside potential of $20 million+ according to the earnings call. Meanwhile, this deal is expected to start generating meaningful recurring software subscription revenue in 2026.

In my view, the magnitude of this win must not be underestimated especially in light of how other sectoral peers are faring. American Well Corporation (NYSE:AMWL), or Amwell, a SaaS-based technology-enabled healthcare platform provider, during the same period had its U.S. Defense Health Agency (DHA), a division of the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) that manages the Military Health System (MHS), contract being extended by only a year instead of the expected three years plus a nine-month transition.

Claritev's wins are more pronounced with renewals at five years and 10-years+. That's quite impressive, isn't it?

In addition, the company also signed new logos across multiple market segments during the second quarter. These included five in the payer/Third Party Administrator (TPA) space and three in the provider market. Meanwhile, partnerships with Oracle, Athena, and Burjeel are still producing minimal income. What that implies is that execution will be the critical hurdle for CTEV, I think.

Overall, there's a good story to tell as far as Claritev's operations are concerned.

Insider Trading

CTEV's top management continued with more purchases of its own stock.

In May, purchases by the top three executives amounted to $1.18 million, led by CFO Garis Douglas Michael, COO Hogge Jerome, and CEO Travis Dalton. The stock price at that time was averaging around $25.

That said, between August 7-11, further purchases by Travis and Jerome amounting to a total of $549 million is a sign of confidence in the company on the part of top leadership. Most noteworthy, is the stock prices at which these latest August purchases took place at which were averaging around $55. Top executives were and are willing to trade CTEV stock at a premium of over 120% from that price of three-months ago. They should be seeing something great in their operations, right?

Leverage Plus Negative Equity: CTEV's Major Headache

Despite all the positives, the company's debt load and high interest burden remain heavy overhangs. Shareholder equity has been on a sustained decline and for the past seven consecutive quarters (the ones under review below, could be longer) highlighted, the current quarter is the first for the group to have a negative figure at -$39.9 million.

As a result, the debt/equity ratio now stands at -11358%. This is an indisputable red flag as it signals extreme leverage and a fragile balance sheet. Such levels I believe are common in companies recovering from years of losses.

That said, I might say hope is not lost but a lot of hard work is still required. The good thing is on the direction at which metrics like EBITDA and margin growths are taking. In addition, recurring revenue products like Ben Insights are critical in turning around such a bad situation.

The company also highlighted that the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act” is also boosting free cash flow. To me that will also help in reducing debt going forward.

On the other hand, pending lawsuits amounting to a much as $25.4 billion in potential payouts is a risk worth noting.

Valuation

CTEV's EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.13x, below the healthcare technology industry average of 14.14x, is suggesting that it may be modestly undervalued relative to peers. However, it sits above Cognizant (NASDAQ: CTSH), a major competitor, which trades at 9.15x. I believe this positioning implies the market assigns CTEV a valuation premium over certain established competitors. Possibly, I think this reflects its high-margin growth potential, expanding subscription revenue base, and turnaround progress.

At the same time, its discount to the broader industry could indicate lingering concerns over its leveraged balance sheet and execution risks. Overall, the valuation suggests cautious optimism pricing in both opportunity and the risks tied to its financial structure and strategic transformation.

Conclusion

CTEV stock's price surge for the year now at over 375%, and at over 155% for the past six months are scary numbers for me given the other risks associated. I acknowledge valid reasons for the surges but I feel the magnitude is just too high given problems like high leverage, and now the negative equity. Yes, the prospects for a 'turn' are still there but this stock performance so far seems too much for me. I choose to sit back a bit, or Hold and watch. What are your thoughts?

data.title: XXRP Can Provide 2x Exposure To XRP
data.description:
The Teucrium 2x Long Daily XRP ETF (NYSEARCA:XXRP) is a leveraged strategy that seeks to achieve 2x the daily performance of Ripple (XRP-USD). Given that this is a leveraged strategy, XXRP should only be used by active traders and should not be used as a long-term investment strategy.

XRP Background

XRP is one of the most heavily traded cryptocurrencies, with recent volumes being in the $11 billion per day range. XRP Ledger was launched in 2012 as an open-source, permissionless and decentralized technology designed to enhance transaction speed. XRP has historically received substantial support across financial institutions aiming to enhance efficiency, reduce transaction time, and offer an alternative for international payment services.

XRP recently had a major win against a 2020 legal case by the SEC. At the time, the SEC accused Ripple of selling XRP as an unregistered security. In 2023, a federal judge ruled that XRP sold on public exchanges did not meet the definition of a security. On August 7, 2025, the lawsuit was abandoned with a $125 million fine to the SEC.

XXRP Features

XXRP is a leveraged investment strategy that provides traders 2x the daily performance of XRP. XXRP does this by holding a portfolio of XRP futures, providing the appropriate leveraged position to achieve the 2x daily performance.

Futures are settled on a daily basis, meaning that at the end of each trading day, daily profits and losses determine margin requirements. If losses mount, the counterparty may request additional collateral in good faith of covering at settlement. As a result of daily settlement, performance will reset on a daily basis, making performance metrics meaningful on a daily basis.

This is reflected in XXRP as an investment vehicle in which performance is reset on a daily basis. This means that returns can be compounded over time, resulting in returns above or below the 2x daily performance target. For this reason, XXRP should only be held for no longer than a single trading period. Holding periods longer than a single trading day can also result in value decay.

Comparing single-day performance, XXRP has exhibited significant deviance from its 2x daily performance target, potentially making this ETF riskier than peer leveraged strategies.

To the upside, XXRP may outperform its 2x daily target.

Considering daily variation, there are many variables that go into performance, including volatility and trading volumes. XXRP has relatively strong depth with $462 million in assets under management with an average of 1.11 million shares changing hands on a daily basis. Spread risk, or cost-in/cost-out, is relatively high at 27bps. In addition to this, XXRP has a relatively high expense ratio of 189bps, a significant cost when compared to single-stock leveraged strategies managed by firms like ProShares, Amplify, or Direxion.

Risks Related to Leveraged Strategies

There are inherent risks in investing in leveraged strategies. Since these strategies are meant to achieve their stated multiple over the underlying index on a daily basis, holding these strategies beyond a single day can lead to skewed performance, especially following consecutive days of upside or downside risk. This can lead to a compounding effect as well as value decay as a result of the daily reset in the derivatives positions.

Leveraged strategies also add an extra layer of risk to amplified downside potential. For example, if the underlying index were to appreciate by 2% in a single day, a 3x strategy would appreciate by 6%, meaning that an investment of $100 would increase to $106. If the following day results in a -2% decline in the underlying index, or a -6% decline in the leveraged strategy, the investment will decline to $99.64.

Holding leveraged strategies for longer than a single day can lead to a compounding effect on one’s position, potentially leading to significant skews from the intended multiplier. During multiple days of downside risk, an investor can experience significant value decay, potentially resulting in the inability to recover losses.

Liquidity must be considered before investing in a leveraged fund strategy. Given that these funds are designed to be traded daily, trading volumes must be considered to ensure that cost-in/cost-out isn’t deteriorated by spreads.

Traders must also consider additional taxes on short-term capital gains as well as trading fees before considering a leveraged trading strategy. You can review additional risks here.

Before making a trading or investment decision in leveraged strategies, be sure to fully understand your risk profile and set a trading mandate to manage these risks. I believe preemptively setting parameters for selling out of a position is prudent in ensuring that an investor appropriately manages downside risk.

Final Thoughts

XXRP is a relatively liquid strategy designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of XRP. Given that this is a leveraged strategy, it should not be considered as a long-term investment vehicle and should only be used for daily exposure. I'm recommending XXRP with a "Hold" rating.

data.title: Eli Lilly: Bet Against Insiders
data.description:
LLY stock: unusual insider purchases

I last covered Eli Lilly and Company (NYSE:LLY) about 2 years ago in August 2023. In an article titled “Eli Lilly Q2: Dividends Don't Lie”, I rated the stock as a buy given the potential of its pipeline drug, in particular in the obesity space. Since, the company’s stock prices have indeed enjoyed a terrific ride. Shortly afterward, Eli Lilly was considered the first pharmaceutical stock with the potential to break the one-trillion-dollar market cap. Its stock price soared to nearly $1,000 in 2024, with a market value exceeding $910 billion as seen in the chart below.

However, the stock has been pressured lately. Especially last week, after the release of its quarterly earnings report for Q2 2025, the stock price pulled by over 15% at one point. All told, the company is now valued around $650 billion, and about $260 billion worth of market worth has been wiped out since the high point observed in 2024.

Against this background, the latest insiders’ activities stand in stark contrast to the selling pressure in the open market. To be more explicit, the following insider disclosures (provided by DataRoma) reveal several insider purchases in August shortly after the earnings report. As seen, there were a total of 8 buy transactions disclosed in the past 3 months, and all were executed since Aug 8. These purchases totaled over $4 million dollars and were disclosed by high-profile management including directors and EVPs.

LLY stock: why do the insider buys stand out?

LLY’s above-mentioned purchases are also unusual when contextualized by the insider activities over the broader market. As an example, the next chart shows the net amount of insider transactions in the past 2 months in the overall equity market. As seen, the picture is totally dominated by sellers (i.e., the red bars), which dwarf the buyers so much that the green bars are hardly visible.

Also note that the above insider purchases were mostly executed in a price range between $640 and $660 per share. The current price of LLY stock is around $690, about 5% to 8% above the purchase prices from the insiders. Next, I will explain why I do not see these prices as attractive entry points.

LLY stock is still overvalued

Despite the large price pullback, LLY stock is still overvalued by a large margin in my view. As an example, when compared to its sector, Eli Lilly receives an overall valuation grade of D-. The company's current P/E ratio (on a Non-GAAP and TTM basis) is 42.38x, quite elevated both in relative and absolute terms. It is significantly higher than the sector median of 16.72, representing a 153.53% premium. Of course, compared to its own historical level, the current P/E is at a notable discount. For example, when compared to its five-year averages, the current P/E ratios suggest a discount in the range of around -25% to -31%. However, I believe the historical averages are severely inflated due to the market expectations for weight-loss drugs in the past few years.

This leads me to LLY’s oral weight-loss drug GLP-1, Orforglipron. It was the market’s focal point during the recent earnings release and also a key reason behind my bullish thinking in my last writing. However, judging by the latest clinical trial results, I believe the competing landscape has changed materially since my last writing. For example, in tandem with the earnings report, Eli Lilly also updated the Phase 3 clinical trial data for this drug in early August. The trial results were robust in themselves when adjusted for placebo effects, they did not appear competitive enough in my view when compared to other weight-loss drugs such as semaglutide.

With the shifted competitive landscape, I see large odds for LLY’s stock price and margins to remain under pressure. As a reflection, the chart below shows my FWD projection of LLY’s net margin. Based on the Q2 quarterly earnings (announced on August 7), the company's normalized EPS dialed in $6.31 on total revenue of $15.56 billion. Based on these figures, the normalized net margin for the latest quarter was 36.4%. Looking ahead, the upcoming quarter's earnings (to be announced on October 30, 2025) point to a normalized EPS of $6.35 on total revenue of $16.06 billion. These estimates translate into an implied net margin of 35.6% assuming the same number of shares outstanding, a compression of 80 basis points. Looking further out, the margin pressure could persist or even worsen for reasons detailed next.

Other risks and final thoughts

To further compound the valuation and profitability risks, LLY - and the overall healthcare sector - is facing considerable pricing uncertainties, at least in the US market. As a reflection of these uncertainties, President Trump recently sent an ultimatum to 17 leading pharmaceutical manufacturers, demanding them to “take steps to lower drug prices in the U.S. within 60 days in line with his most-favored-nation drug pricing policy”. LLY is one of the companies that received this ultimatum. It is not clear to me what pricing changes LLY and/or the sector as a whole would propose in response to the ultimatum. But I see large odds for LLY and other companies to accommodate the “most favored nation” (MFN) pricing concept.

Of course, there are a few key positives here too. At a macro level, weight-loss drugs indeed enjoy a tremendous addressable market. According to the 2025 World Obesity Atlas, the percentage of the population with overweight and/or obesity conditions will keep increasing as you can see from the following chart. Combined with the population increase itself, my estimate is that by 2030, over 2.9 billion adults worldwide will have a high body mass index (BMI). Against the secular trend, it is no surprise that weight-loss drugs have experienced explosive growth in recent years, and I expect the growth to sustain in the years to come. Specific to LLY, it might be able to raise prices elsewhere to offset the price pressure in the US market. For example, in the earnings report, it has announced a plan to raise GLP1 pricing in the UK by a healthy margin, essentially doubling the price there.

All told, this follow-up on LLY is mainly triggered by two recent catalysts: the latest purchases disclosed by insiders and also the clinical trial results of its GLP1. After examining these changes, my conclusion is that I am not seeing any obvious skewness in its return/risk curve under the current prices or the prices the insiders made their recent purchases.

data.title: SDS: Tactical Tool, But Only For Agile Traders Who Can Bear The Risks
data.description:
Introduction

The ProShares UltraShort S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SDS) is a 2x leveraged inverse fund that aims to provide -200% of the daily return of the underlying index, the S&P 500. The fund is used for nimble traders to profit from a market decline or other negative market movements, and the leveraged factor certainly makes it very risky for long-term holds. Below are some key facts of the fund, taken from the prospectus.

Expense Ratio: 0.89%

Trading Volume: 18.5M

AUM: $460M

Within the underlying index, here are the top holdings.

We believe that over time, the market overall should increase in value. This fund should only be used for active traders to play short-term catalysts that are negative in the market. Below, we will review some of the broader trends and recent news that can impact the markets.

Broader Market Trends

Tariff situation remains not resolved

Overall, the markets have rebounded significantly since the depths of the April tariff volatility, and many countries and important trading partners of the US have already signed deals or are working on finalizing the frameworks for their deals. The biggest trading partner, China, remains not finalized and was recently given another 90 days to come to a resolution. With this new November 10th deadline, both countries are going to be able to hopefully come to an agreement without feeling too pressured by time and therefore, can work out a deal that better suits both parties - including trade, AI chips, rare earth metals, and other sticking points of the overall US and China relationship. Another big partner for the US that currently remains in flux is India, but signs are pointing to a deal coming to fruition in the fall. However these major deals play out, there will be opportunities to trade the volatility that may occur from announcements on trade - and SDS would be a good option to play the market swings.

Fed may look to cut interest rates soon

The latest CPI data this week showed the markets that the battle with inflation has appeared to come to a reasonable close - at the minimum, enough for the Fed to finally start adjusting rates lower in their upcoming September meeting. With CPI up 2.7% annually versus an estimate of 2.8%, there is growing hope amongst investors that the Fed will ultimately cut, and this should spur growth, investment, and more capital infused into the markets. We view this as a bullish sign for the markets overall, but again emphasize that leveraged broader market funds like SDS can be used to play volatility in the markets, and any sign that the Fed remains hesitant to cut could lead to a negative market reaction. Further, with the ongoing jabs that the Trump administration is levying on Jerome Powell, such as threatening to sue over the renovations taking place at the headquarters of the Fed, these are also examples of news that could move the markets (if Trump did indeed remove Powell).

Per the graph above, we believe that the Fed does have room to lower rates, as they are near 10 year highs, but it ultimately remains to be seen what the Fed decides to do. This will be an upcoming catalyst that could present sharp traders with an opportunity.

AI investments are still going strong

There is still an influx of capital going into AI companies and infrastructure, and short of a rotation out of the sector entirely, these AI investments should have enough weight to carry the markets forward. With hundreds of billions of dollars worth of investment just from the Big tech players this year, there remains a tidal wave of investment activity that could propel the markets further. Estimates state that AI investment has added $152B to the GDP in the first half of the year, and we see no reason that this trend will not continue for the near to mid term future. With governmental involvement and support as well, this entire sector and the various companies in the market that cater to AI infrastructure and development should benefit. Additional AI related announcements and developments could also move the markets, proving to be catalysts for traders.

Risks of Leverage

It is important to understand the risks that come with a leveraged fund such as SDS. We will explore the risks through a few different lenses.

Loss Recoupment

When it comes to loss recoupment, we try to understand what needs to happen to breakeven after your investment loses value. On a dollar basis the answer is easy: If you lose $5, you need to gain back $5 to breakeven. However on a percentage basis it’s not as simple. If you lose 15% you need to gain back 17.65% to breakeven. Similarly, for any percentage loss, the percentage gain required for breakeven is always higher. For most investments, the percentage basis does not matter as much because the more important metric is the dollar value of your investment.

However for a leveraged ETF, this risk looks different. This is for 2 reasons:

  • The performance of a leveraged ETF is fully dependant on the underlying security it is tracking

  • A leveraged ETF tracks the underlying security on a percentage basis. In the case of SDS, it will have -2x the percentage movement of the S&P 500 during any given trading day.

As a result we need to change our loss recoupment analysis to be based on the underlying security’s movement AND also look at it on a percentage basis rather than a dollar basis.

Let’s start with a non-leveraged investment on a percentage basis:

We see that, as expected, the % gain required for breakeven is always higher than the percentage loss incurred. However, we also see that the % gain grows faster than the % loss so the higher the % loss, the larger the % gain (both on an absolute basis and also relative to the % loss) will need to be.

Now let's add the line that represents a 2x leveraged ETF:

The red line here shows the % gain on the underlying security (in this case the S&P 500) required to offset a % loss on the underlying security for holders of a 2x leveraged fund on the security.

Note: Since SDS is an inverse leveraged ETF, the labels here are technically wrong. When the S&P goes up, SDS will go down and vice versa. So instead we are actually looking at the % loss required on the S&P to breakeven from a % gain on the S&P. However, in the interest of comparing the 2 charts and because the math works out the same either way, we leave it as is.

We see that not only is the red line higher than the blue line, it also grows much faster. This is why leveraged ETFs have a much higher risk than other investments. The main point here is that % losses will always outweigh equivalent % gains, and this is especially true for leveraged ETFs.

Inverse Leverage

Because SDS is an inverse ETF, there is a disadvantage over non-inverse ETFs. Yes, the % loss required to offset a % gain on the S&P will always be higher, but from a dollar basis, it is preferable to have a non-inverse ETF. Let’s look at 2 scenarios to explore this:

  • Assume we have 2 leveraged ETFs: One that is 2x leveraged on the S&P and one that is -2x (inverse) leveraged on the S&P

  • Assume that the S&P is currently at $100

Scenario

Day 1 S&P Movement

Day 1 Leverage Movement

Day 2 S&P Movement Required for Breakeven

2x Leverage

-10%, -$10

-20%

+12.5%, $11.25

-2x Leverage

+10%, +$10

-20%

-12.5%, -$13.75

We see that on a percentage basis, the 2 scenarios are equivalent (but opposite). However, on a dollar basis, the inverse ETF needs the S&P 500 to move by $13.75, while the non-inverse ETF only needs the S&P to move by $11.25.

This is because in the case of the inverse ETF, the S&P 500 moved up in value on day 1 to $110 while in the non-inverse ETF case the S&P 500 moved down to $90. So even though the percentage movement for both is equivalent at 12.5%, the inverse case is working off of a larger starting number.

Volatility

One point related to loss recoupment to mention is volatility. During periods of time where there are many ups and downs, the gains on a leveraged ETF will likely suffer. On a daily basis, SDS will always move by -2x the movement of the S&P 500. However, if you hold SDS for longer than 1 day, your returns may differ from -2x as compounding takes effect. As we saw earlier, the impacts of losses are always larger than the impacts of gains on a leveraged ETF. So when you have periods of time with volatility, the negative days will have a larger impact and will drive down returns on the leveraged ETF.

Conclusion

There are some major broader market trends and catalysts that can continue to influence and sway the S&P. Resolution of the trade war, a rate cut, and AI investments will all continue to be market-moving events and news. There could be an opportunity to use SDS strategically and tactically to play a negative reaction.

However, we advise that investors remain cautious and strategic when it comes to SDS. As a leveraged ETF it comes with higher than normal risks, and as an inverse leveraged ETF, the risks are even higher. As ProShares states:

The Fund uses leverage and will lose more money when the value of the Index rises than a similar fund that does not use leverage. The use of leverage increases the risk of a total loss of your investment. If the Index approaches a 50% gain at any point in the day, you could lose your entire investment.

Overall, we recommend SDS as a short-term tactical investment that is utilized only for short periods of time. Good times to consider SDS are when there are potential market catalysts that you believe will drive down the value of the S&P 500. Examples include anticipated interest rate increases, announcements of new tariffs or a less than stellar jobs report. Either way, remain cautious and ensure you fully understand the risks before making any investments in SDS.

data.title: Hey Boomer: Wanna Know What Gen-Z Thinks About Brokerages?
data.description:
Manole Capital Management

7th Annual Gen-Z Survey: Brokerages

August 2025

Introduction:

Manole Capital Management is a boutique asset manager based in Tampa, Florida, exclusively focused on the emerging FINTECH sector. We define FINTECH as “anything utilizing technology to improve an established process.”

For the past seven years, Manole Capital has tasked its interns with researching the Gen-Z demographic. The goal of this annual study is to better understand Gen-Z’s knowledge of and perspectives on financial services. Each year, we examine opinions and trends across four key verticals: digital currencies, brokerage, banking, and payments. Earlier studies are available on Sum Zero.

2025 Interns:

This year’s research was conducted by interns from a diverse set of colleges and universities, including:

  • David Gaffen (Yeshiva University)
  • Edward Rochte (Indiana University)
  • Isaish Lu (University of North Carolina)
  • Mia Stavola (Florida Atlantic University)

Participants:

The 2025 survey includes insights from a broad and diverse group of respondents:

  • 94% identify as Gen-Z
  • 58% identify as male, 41% as female
  • 88% are based in the US and 12% are international

Responses were gathered from students at over 100 different colleges and universities.

Defining the Generations:

To provide context for Gen-Z's attitudes and behaviors, it is important to contrast them with prior generations:

Baby Boomers (1946–1964): Grew up in a pre-digital world, value personal relationships, and tend to be loyal to financial institutions (averaging 15 years per relationship). They are optimistic and hardworking and are now entering retirement.

Gen-X (1965–1980): Often called the “lost generation,” Gen-Xers tend to be self-reliant and value work-life balance. Raised during shifting family dynamics, they are skeptical but resourceful.

Millennials (1981–1996): The first generation to fully embrace digital technology. While tech-savvy and driven, they can be overly reliant on technology. Raised by Gen-X parents, they were afforded more flexibility, sometimes leading to generational misunderstandings.

Why Study Gen-Z?

With over two billion people globally, Gen-Z is more than 25% of the world’s population and is quickly becoming one of its most influential economic forces.

In the U.S., Gen-Z makes up 70 million people and is expected to account for over a quarter of the global workforce by 2025. In 2024, Gen-Z earned over $400 billion in the U.S. alone. Some estimates project that Gen-Z will command $40 trillion in buying power by 2035. In the coming decades, Gen-Z will inherit more than $70 trillion from older generations in the largest wealth transfer in history.

Gen-Z’s digital-first mindset is reshaping how people bank, invest, and spend. Understanding this cohort’s evolving behavior is critical for financial institutions, investors, and portfolio managers looking to stay ahead of future trends.

Optimism:

We started our survey by asking, "Are you positive or negative about the US economy?”

We expected Gen-Z to be generally optimistic about the US economy, predicting responses to lean heavily positive (around 75% to 80%).

However, our results did not show this kind of optimistic outlook. While 60% of respondents viewed the US economy positively, 40% had a negative view. This is the same response we received in last year's survey when we asked the same question.

In the sections below, you will find each question that the Brokerage team asked of its respondents, as well as our opinions and big-picture takeaways from our survey results.

Question #1: Do you have a brokerage account?

Of the Gen-Zer’s surveyed, 52% answered yes. An additional 20% said they don't have one yet but want to open one. The remaining 28% are not interested at all in opening one. This shows that over half of Gen-Z already have investments in the markets, and nearly three-quarters are either invested or planning to invest.

Compared to last year, when 56% had a brokerage account, this figure is modestly down. This slight decline could reflect broader financial pressures such as rising living costs, university expenses, and the appeal of fixed bank rates. The 28% of those who are not willing to open an account is similar to last year and not surprising.

Back in 2018, during our first survey of Gen-Z, only 17% had a brokerage account. Now, with 52% of Gen-Z having a brokerage account, the long-term growth is still notable. Overall, Gen-Z is a generation that has adopted investing, and the future looks bright for those firms that target Gen-Z.

Question #2: If you do NOT have a brokerage account, why not?

  • 40% Plan to start soon
  • 32% Not enough money to invest
  • 14% Prefer to save
  • 12% Too risky
  • 2% Don’t trust banks/apps

This shows promise. The fact that nearly half of non-investors are preparing to open accounts shows future potential for the different brokerage accounts to gain a material number of users. Affordability is still a key barrier, consistent with past surveys, especially in times of rising inflation and student debt. The “too risky” group reflects ongoing concerns that markets can be volatile and intimidating, particularly without proper education.

For brokerages, the message is clear: offering more affordable investment options could reduce the financial barriers to entry for the next generation.

Question #3: Which brokerage are you with?

  • 32% Schwab + Ameritrade
  • 24% Robinhood
  • 15% Fidelity
  • 11% E*TRADE (owned by MS)
  • 6% JP Morgan Chase
  • 6% Vanguard
  • 6% Merrill Lynch (owned by B of A)

Gen-Z’s brokerage preferences saw a clear shift in 2025. Robinhood, which held the top spot in 2024 with 31%, dropped to 24% this year — a 7-point decline after three consecutive years as the leader.

Schwab/Ameritrade, previously at 29%, jumped to 32%, making it the new #1 platform among Gen-Z. Fidelity emerged as a major player this year at 15%, even though it wasn’t listed individually in last year’s results. Meanwhile, E*TRADE grew slightly to keep a solid position, while legacy platforms like JP Morgan, Vanguard, and Merrill Lynch each held steady at 6%.

This reshuffling highlights a broader trend: Gen-Z is diversifying their brokerage choices. While Robinhood’s drop suggests that younger investors may be looking for more robust features beyond sleek app design, Schwab and Fidelity appear to be benefiting from offering more comprehensive financial tools and resources. The data also shows that no single platform holds majority loyalty, instead, Gen-Z is experimenting, comparing, and customizing their investing experience across multiple platforms.

Question #4: What factors influenced your brokerage decision? (select up to 3)

Now that we’ve found where Gen-Z has its brokerage account, we want to better understand what factors influenced that major decision.

  • 23% Ease of use
  • 19% Brand reputation
  • 18% No commissions/fees
  • 18% Word of mouth
  • 11% Integration with bank accounts
  • 11% Investment options variety

These responses reflect a generation that wants investing to be convenient, simple, and intuitive. The focus on a user-friendly interface shows that design and simplicity are central to Gen-Z's investing habits. Brand image is equally important, as trust plays a key role in where money is invested.

That a third of respondents were influenced by word of mouth or brand reputation says a lot about how much Gen-Z trusts what people say - whether friends, parents, or social media. Money decisions are not made in isolation. They are driven by relationships and experiences.

Other features like banking integration or robo-advisory tools are secondary, but that could change. As apps like Robinhood and others start offering AI-enabled research and personalized insights, Gen-Z might use them. Right now, the winning combination is clear: a simple-to-use app with a well-known brand that someone they trust recommends.

Question #5: Is mobile trading sufficient, or would you like the option to trade on a desktop or laptop?

We know Gen-Z loves their mobile phones. We wanted to understand whether trading on their mobile phone is sufficient, or if they would prefer to have the option of trading on a desktop or laptop.

When asked about trading platforms, 71% said that mobile trading is sufficient. The remaining 29% expressed a preference for the option to trade on a larger device, like a desktop or laptop. This shows how highly Gen-Z values mobile-first platforms. With most respondents finding mobile trading enough, it is clear that usability and accessibility drive engagement. A large majority of young investors are comfortable managing their portfolios on smartphones, where clean design and real-time alerts are standard.

Nevertheless, nearly a third were interested in laptop or desktop capability. This group might be more experienced traders with access to sophisticated software, tabs, or larger data screens. It might also be students or professionals who are already on computers all day and find desktop trading more convenient for research or execution. The figures show that while mobile-first will stay important, platforms that offer both desktop and mobile features can serve a larger segment of Gen-Z users as their investment skills grow.

Question #6: If you have an account at Robinhood, please identify what is attractive with “Robinhood Gold”?

This year, we added a question to evaluate whether Robinhood’s premium offering - “ Robinhood Gold”- resonates with Gen-Z investors.

  • 34% High interest on uninvested cash
  • 21% Not familiar with Robinhood Gold
  • 18% Margin buying power
  • 18% Research tools
  • 9% Don’t see value in it

Our main takeaway:

Among Gen-Z users, the 5% interest rate was the most appealing benefit. Clearly, Gen-Z likes getting a bit of interest on their cash balances. In our opinion, Robinhood might be better off if they clearly and concisely communicated this yield advantage (versus traditional banks). Robinhood Gold seems to be a product offering that Gen-Z and hungry yield investors would be attracted to.

Tools like margin buying power and research features appeal to some, but a clear education and awareness gap still exists around the premium service. For Robinhood, this presents an opportunity to better communicate the tangible benefits of Gold and convert more free users into paying subscribers.

Question #7: What investments do you have? (select all that apply)

To better understand how Gen-Z allocates capital - within their portfolios, we asked what investments they currently have.

  • 40% Stocks
  • 24% Mutual funds / ETFs
  • 19% Crypto
  • 10% Bonds
  • 7% Options

Stocks rose from 37% last year to 40%, staying the top investment for Gen-Z. ETFs and mutual funds stayed roughly the same as last year (from 25% to 24%), while crypto jumped from 13% to 19%. Clearly, the substantial increase in digital asset prices this year has resulted in a higher percentage of Gen-Z’s portfolio.

These shifts suggest Gen-Z is becoming more confident and active in managing diverse portfolios. Stocks, ETFs, and crypto remain Gen-Z’s top picks because they blend long-term growth with innovation. While stocks and ETFs offer stability and diversification, crypto reflects Gen-Z’s appetite for tech-driven, high-potential opportunities.

Question #8: Which social media platform do you follow for financial advice?

With Gen-Z using social media every day, we wanted to understand if Gen-Z relied on it for financial advice.

A clear majority (43%) of respondents reported not using any social media for financial advice, indicating a strong preference for either traditional financial education (books, advisors, news) or skepticism about online financial content.

Surprising to some, this aligns with broader trends showing a significant trust gap with social media as a credible financial resource, especially among older demographics or financially conservative users.

TikTok leads the pack among those who use social platforms for financial advice. TikTok was the most popular at 17%, highlighting the rising influence of “FinTok” — short, easy-to-understand, personality-driven finance content mainly aimed at Gen-Z and younger Millennials. Some of its dominance can be attributed to a shift toward quick advice and influencer trust, rather than traditional institutional messaging.

Information Hubs:

  • YouTube (13%) offers a more in-depth, tutorial-stye form and appeals to those seeking visual walkthroughs and long-form explanations (e.g., investing strategies, market trends, budgeting tools).
  • X (Twitter) (11%) remains popular for real-time insights, threads from analysts, and financial news, especially among tech-savvy or finance-curious users.

Niche Influence:

  • Instagram (10%) serves as a more aesthetic, influencer-oriented finance space - useful for motivation, infographics, and daily tips, but less so for deep dives.
  • Reddit (5%) remains a community-driven knowledge pool, particularly strong for crowd-sourced discussions (e.g., r/personalfinance, r/wallstreetbets).
  • Facebook (1%) is barely used for finance, signaling its continued decline in relevance among younger, investment-minded users.

Question #9: Do you trade options?

Options trading has become an increasingly popular strategy for those seeking the thrill of higher returns. Therefore, we wanted to explore how Gen-Z currently views this more advanced investing choice.

Here’s how respondents answered:

No: 78%

  • 14% No, too risky
  • 13% No, too complex
  • 51% Don’t know enough about options

Yes: 22%

  • 13% Yes, for higher returns
  • 8% Yes, for leverage
  • 1% Yes, I love 0-day options!

Options trading saw a noticeable uptick among Gen-Z investors, with 22% saying they trade options in 2025 (last year was only 14%). While 78% still don’t trade options, the increase points to growing curiosity and rising interest in more advanced strategies. Although momentum is building, this suggests that options still haven’t fully made their way into Gen-Z investment strategies just yet.

Our takeaway?

There’s still a wide educational gap, but momentum is building. As more accessible platforms and tools emerge, options may become a larger part of Gen-Z’s investment toolkit.

Conclusion:

Many Gen-Zer’s are already investing, but they are cautious about the economy and the cost of living. Still, many who haven't already opened a brokerage account plan to begin soon. This underscores the significant opportunity to expand brokerage account ownership among Gen-Z.

Similar to previous years, key factors affecting which brokerage they utilize are ease of use, a trusted brand, and no trading fees. Schwab + Ameritrade regained the top option (according to our survey and research), but Gen-Z still loves their mobile phone and Robinhood’s user interface. It appears that Gen-Z is not necessarily committed to one single platform.

Recent technology has given people the ability to trade on the go, and Gen-Z seems to prefer to trade on their mobile phone. However, some want the choice to use a computer as well.

Our main takeaway?

Research and education are a necessity. Gen-Z feels like they do not have enough money and are worried about the affordability of college and living expenses. Some view investing as too risky, or are struggling to grasp concepts like options trading. For any financial company trying to attract this up-and-coming generation, the message is clear - make investing simple, affordable, and provide education for our journey.

Thanks for reading our research and we hope you have a wonderful summer!

The Manole Capital 2025 Summer Intern Team

data.title: UnitedHealth: Berkshire Bets, A Strategic Signal Investors Can't Ignore
data.description:
The Berkshire Hathaway circle of competence

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) is in rally mode after a sizable purchase by Warren Buffett and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B, BRK.A). While the purchase is not going to put the stock into the top 10 holdings for Berkshire, everyone should be paying attention when Berkshire Hathaway makes a purchase squarely in their circle of competence.

Sectors the privately held operating businesses are in

For those that own Berkshire Hathaway and know the business well, they know that it consists of the well know portfolio of publicly listed securities and the private operating businesses. Those operating businesses consist of:

  • Insurance and re-insurance.
  • Rail and transportation.
  • Energy/utility companies through Berkshire Hathaway Energy.

The 13F

We can see the 3 new buys in the Berkshire portfolio that the company claimed special treatment for priorly were:

  • Lennar (LEN)
  • Nucor (NUE)
  • UnitedHealth.

The largest purchase of the 3 was UnitedHealth at $1.572 billion.

18th largest on the list

This, however, does not get the company even into the top 10 of Berkshire Hathaway holdings, and it does not also mean that they are done buying either.

Insurance is Berkshire Hathaway's premiere expertise

For those that read the Berkshire Hathaway letters and attend or listen to the meetings, they know that the Vice Chairman of the insurance operations, Ajit Jain, is heralded as the reason Berkshire's insurance operations became so profitable. Warren Buffett will tell you that there may not be another individual as knowledgeable and sharp at underwriting in the world than Ajit Jain. This purchase in UnitedHealth, while not massive, would definitely have the due diligence assistance of Ajit. I can not imagine this purchase being made without his insight.

How cheap is UnitedHealth?

Pre-market August 15th, 2025, the stock remained about -56% off of it's all time high. This crash of the one blue chip insurance stalwart in the Dow 30 (DIA) is not without reason, I have covered that topic in depth in my previous article as have several other analysts. I look at the margin between the all-time high and where we currently sit as meat on the bone. If we ever return to that all-time high, that's about 127% in upside potential not to mention the total return profile from receiving the dividend all along the way.

Now that Berkshire may be putting a floor under this one at around $300/share, for those willing to wait, even a 3-year time horizon of sitting and collecting the dividend would almost certainly be a market beating performance should this stock ever return to its old multiples and earnings profile.

UNH Vs. historical ratios

As we can see historically, UnitedHealth traded at a low to mid 20s P/E ratio for almost the entire decade outside of Covid. If we look back even further in the charts, there is no re-rating of this stock's multiple as dramatic as the one we are in right now:

The drawdown post 2006 was close, that was related to a stock options investigation, but not on the same level as this acute cliff that the stock has fallen off this time.

It's not just UnitedHealth in a difficult situation

S&P Global recently re-rated the entire health insurance sector from stable to negative:

We revised our U.S. health insurance sector view to negative from stable. Key drivers include:

• The recent and projected strain in operating performance, predominantly in the Medicare Advantage and Medicaid segments, and in certain geographic markets;

• A rising share of negative rating outlooks (35% as of Jan. 29, 2025) based on company-specific earnings deterioration and criteria changes; and

• Higher legislative and regulatory risk, which could affect both health insurance and pharmacy-related segments.

Fitch also chimed in with their downgrade of the sector:

The outlook change for U.S. health insurers reflects ongoing higher growth in healthcare utilization and increasing crystallization around the form and magnitude of cuts to government-funded healthcare programs, primarily Medicaid, incorporated into the ‘One Big Beautiful Bill Act’. The bill was passed by the House of Representatives on May 22 and is currently with the Senate, where potential changes could either mitigate or exacerbate its impact on the Medicaid program.

We always have to remember that Presidential administrations last for about 4 years unless re-elected with the same political mandate. One mandate rarely carries over from one term to the next. Government cuts to Medicaid are sure to hurt the entire healthcare sector, but deportations of some individuals that would have had access to Medicaid otherwise might create some offsets.

Government funding and subsidies to healthcare 3.5 years from now will probably look a lot different than they do today. There will most likely be a few years of poor performance amongst the group. However, a pullback here is also an opportunity for patient investors. Several other family offices are joining along in this bet with Berkshire Hathaway and I'm sure they have the liquidity and patience to see this through. If you have money you can put away in this bet for a few years, it seems like a historical opportunity.

How is the balance sheet looking

The credit profile is a negative aspect of UnitedHealth, long-term debt has risen while EBITDA has taken a big hit. The company now has a long term debt to EBITDA ratio that is just over 3 X. Three times EBITDA is about the maximum debt level you would want to see from a safe balance sheet. Shares outstanding have been falling in the meantime. The company is not hoarding cash, which shows they must have confidence that EBITDA will recover.

The dividend versus history

The dividend is also being paid, with no market rumors of a cut that I've stumbled upon. We can see from the 10 year dividend yield chart that an investor in this last decade has never been able to buy UnitedHealth with a dividend as big as it is today.

The FWD 3.25% yield is a forward payout of $8.84/share, let's see how well covered that is for now.

On a per share basis, the company has $27.6/share in free cash flow TTM. This would be a payout ratio of free cash flow of 32%, even less than the GAAP EPS payout. You would have to retreat back to 2016 levels of free cash flow before the dividend is not covered. Could happen, but the chances of it not happening are in the investor's favor, in my opinion.

Risks

The company will face ongoing legal issues and regulators on Capitol Hill. This is well documented, and I will not discuss those matters in the article. The long-term investor has to weigh their opinion about what they think worst case scenarios and outcomes are regarding this and make their own decisions.

Summary

Having worked in private credit, we looked at financing buildings that had a well known senior lender attached to it, we did not want to be a senior lender on a building that a big bank wouldn't touch. With stocks I also have that same inclination, the banks to us were larger than our operation and we felt they had a greater due diligence capability than our underwriters had. In this case today with UnitedHealth, Berkshire Hathaway, an insurance expert, has determined the stock had a good enough risk reward profile to make it the 18th largest bet in the portfolio.

While Berkshire is not always respected by the market for every pick it makes, when they make a pick where they have expertise as an industry insider, the market should perk up its ears and listen. UnitedHealth is massive, the largest insurance company in the US by revenue. My thesis here is simple, it's a TINA situation, "there is no other alternative." The market cannot absorb all of UnitedHealth's business, and the insurance market cannot survive without UnitedHealth either. Strong Buy.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace: The Space Stock You Should Not Buy
data.description:
Commercializing lunar exploration and travel is a hot topic. However, while many companies are attempting to win a piece of this huge market opportunity, only few have successfully landed on the Moon. Firefly Aerospace (NASDAQ:FLY) is the only recent success story for a commercial company. The company went public in August and with one successful Moon landing, that makes it incredibly interesting to assess the investment appeal for this space company. In this report, I explain why I believe the company’s stock is a sell despite its successful lunar landing.

Unlocking Space: Launch Vehicles And Lunar Landers

While commercializing space and lunar exploration is a hot topic, the backdrop is somewhat grim with a lower budget for NASA. NASA has not walked away from the Artemis program focused on the Moon, but to me it seems that the focus is now more on putting humans on the Moon before China does and some form of planned semi-permanent habitation of the Moon is on the backburner. This could significantly lower the frequency required for missions to the Moon after the first manned mission has taken place. At the same time, NASA is likely to explore closer cooperation with commercial partners and that is where Firefly Aerospace has an advantage.

After its March mission, Firefly Aerospace has become the first commercial company to land its spacecraft in an upright and stable position. The Blue Ghost lander was launched together with the ispace’s Hakuto-R lander in January, but while the landing attempt of the Japanese company failed Firefly Aerospace had a successful landing.

Besides the Blue Ghost lunar lander and its common core Elytra vehicle, FireFly Aerospace is also working on its own launch vehicles. While the Blue Ghost lander was launched on SpaceX’s Falcon 9, the company has its own Firefly Alpha launch vehicle. The track record is not extremely strong with six launches of which two were successful, two were partially successful and two were failure.

Firefly Aerospace is also working on a Medium Launch Vehicle named Eclipse, which is being co-developed with Northrop Grumman (NOC). Firefly Aerospace is also contracted by Northrop Grumman for the development and manufacturing of the upper stage for the Antares 300 series.

What I like about Firefly Aerospace is the following:

  • A combination of launcher development and lunar landers making it more of an end-to-end space company in the future.
  • Customer base including defense prime contractors, such as Lockheed Martin (LMT), Northrop Grumman (NOC) and L3Harris Technologies (LHX).
  • Direct contracts with NASA, the Space Development Agency and the Space Force.

The company has a $1.1 billion backlog including 30 launches and missions and task orders under the Commercial Lunar Payload Services program valued over $400 million.

Space is becoming an increasingly competitive field and that also provides a risk, but the growing demand for commercial and national security space solutions is also rising rapidly providing a significant opportunity for Firefly Aerospace. The image above, shows quite well where each product fits in. The Alpha and Eclipse launch vehicles cover the low-earth to geosynchronous orbits while Elytra allows for satellite deployment from LEO to deep space and the Blue Ghost lander is tailored for the Lunar surface missions.

Firefly Aerospace Raises $868 Million

Firefly Aerospace upsized its IPO to 19.296 million shares priced at $45 per share raising $868 million. Assuming the options to purchase an additional 2.894 million shares are also utilized this would increase the gross proceeds by $130.2 million to $998.6 million. I estimated the net proceeds at $929.25 million.

In its prospectus, the company highlighted a cash pile of $205.3 million, debt of $130.35 million, notes payable held by other parties in the amount of $18.1 million and finance lease liabilities of $3.6 million. This brought the gross debt to $152 million and the net cash position to $53.3 million. The cash pile post IPO would be $1.135 billion

The company also outlined its use case for the proceeds. Part of the proceeds would be used to pay $136.1 million outstanding on the term loan maturing in 2028 and pay $67.7 million in unpaid dividends. This would bring the cash pile to $930.75 million.

For the first six months of the year, the company provided a free cash flow burn figure between $96.5 million and $98.5 million compared to $102.6 million a year ago. Revenues are up from $29.4 million to around $70.4-$71.4 million. But it is still incurring a significant net loss of north of $123.9 million. So, the company has to grow sales further for a break-even on net income and cash flow level. At a burn rate of $50 million per quarter, the company has cash for the coming 4-5 years.

Valuing Firefly Aerospace

Since Firefly Aerospace is not making a profit at this point in time, which is not odd for emerging space companies, I am using an EV/Sales valuation. I am valuing the stock based on two EV/Sales multiples, both are based on Rocket Lab’s multiple. I am choosing Rocket Lab as I consider the company to be the pinnacle of next generation space companies. The first multiple I use is the company median of 12.3x and the second one is the current multiple of 41.9x.

Firefly Aerospace expects H1 revenues to be $71.4 million at the high end. This provides an annualized revenue of $142.8 million. This implies an enterprise value of $1.756 billion. The number of shares outstanding after exercising all stocks available for the capital raise is 146.5 million. This brings the implied stock price to $18.22 per share providing over 61% downside from the current stock price of around $47. Running the same numbers but using a 41.9x multiple brings the implied stock price to $47 implying a fair valuation.

If we incorporate a year of cash burn, the implied stock prices drop to $16.86 and $45.70 respectively. The major challenge for the Firefly Aerospace stock price valuation is the fact that upon offering common stock and paying the preferred dividends, the preferred stock has been converted to common stock. Effectively this means that the preferred stock holders own the largest portion of the company and only a small portion of shares was offered to the public.

Conclusion: Firefly Aerospace Is Not Attractive At This Point

Investment in space is booming at this point and I do believe that Firefly Aerospace has significant potential having some of the biggest defense contractors in its portfolio with a launcher-lander solution in the works as well as a successful landing on the Moon. However, from valuation perspective I believe that the company currently is priced quite high. Even Rocket Lab’s EV/Sales multiple at 41.9x provides no upside for Firefly Aerospace stock. While the company has a successful Moon landing we do note that its launch successes are not as impressive. So, the current stock price is quite high and I believe a stock price substantially below $47 allows for a better entry point. Given the lack of upside even at elevated EV/Sales multiples, I am marking the stock a sell at this point.
